content.text: Major Incident Lessons Program-Wide Rollout

A single team learning from its own incidents is the baseline. An organisation learning from every team's incidents is the multiplier. The major incident lessons program is the mechanism that converts individual postmortems into shared knowledge: deciding which incidents contain lessons relevant beyond the source team, selecting the right rollout channel for each lesson, and verifying that the knowledge actually landed.

The challenge is that not every incident warrants a company-wide discussion, and overwhelming engineers with incident summaries they have no way to act on trains them to ignore the channel. The program must be selective: route lessons to the audiences that can benefit, and only to those audiences.

attrs.alt: Four-step rollout pipeline: 1. Source Team (postmortem authored, action items assigned) → 2. Lessons Triage (EM scores novelty, blast radius, applicability) → 3. Rollout Channels (digest, all-hands, training, game day, runbook update) → 4. Verify (60-day check: knowledge landed?). Channel selection table shows rollout intensity by lesson score from 1-3 (digest only) to 10 (game day + mandatory training + architecture review).

content.text: Lesson Scoring and Triage

Before a lesson is rolled out to any audience, the Engineering Manager who owns the postmortem scores it on three dimensions. The score determines which channels are activated. The scoring takes 15 minutes and is recorded in the postmortem; it does not require a committee.
Dimension
Score 1
Score 2
Score 3
Score 4 (rare)
Novelty
Known failure mode, documented in existing runbook
Known class of failure but new trigger or environment
New failure mode, not previously documented
New failure mode that challenges architectural assumptions
Blast radius
Single team's systems only
Shared infrastructure used by 2–5 teams
Platform or infrastructure used by 6+ teams
Cross-cutting: affects all product surfaces or all users
Applicability
Fix is specific to this team's stack
Pattern applies to teams using similar tooling
Pattern applies to any production service
Lesson should change how the organisation builds and operates

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class RolloutChannel(str, Enum):
    WEEKLY_DIGEST           = "weekly_digest"
    SISTER_TEAM_BRIEFING    = "sister_team_briefing"
    RUNBOOK_UPDATE          = "runbook_update"
    ALL_HANDS_SLOT          = "all_hands_slot"
    ONBOARDING_UPDATE       = "onboarding_update"
    GAME_DAY_EXERCISE       = "game_day_exercise"
    MANDATORY_TRAINING      = "mandatory_training"
    ARCHITECTURE_REVIEW     = "architecture_review"
    SAFETY_POLICY_BRIEFING  = "safety_policy_briefing"

@dataclass
class LessonScore:
    novelty: int        # 1-4
    blast_radius: int   # 1-4
    applicability: int  # 1-4
    safety_incident: bool = False

    def total(self) -> int:
        return self.novelty + self.blast_radius + self.applicability

    def rollout_channels(self) -> list[RolloutChannel]:
        score = self.total()
        channels = [RolloutChannel.WEEKLY_DIGEST]

        if self.safety_incident:
            channels.append(RolloutChannel.SAFETY_POLICY_BRIEFING)

        if score >= 4:
            channels.extend([
                RolloutChannel.SISTER_TEAM_BRIEFING,
                RolloutChannel.RUNBOOK_UPDATE,
            ])

        if score >= 7:
            channels.extend([
                RolloutChannel.ALL_HANDS_SLOT,
                RolloutChannel.ONBOARDING_UPDATE,
            ])

        if score >= 10:
            channels.extend([
                RolloutChannel.GAME_DAY_EXERCISE,
                RolloutChannel.MANDATORY_TRAINING,
                RolloutChannel.ARCHITECTURE_REVIEW,
            ])

        return channels
Rollout Channels in Detail

Each channel serves a different learning objective and a different audience. Activating the wrong channel for a lesson is as wasteful as not rolling out at all: a game day for a lesson that needed only a runbook update consumes two engineering days for negligible additional learning.
Weekly digest:

A short summary (3–5 bullet points) included in the engineering all-hands email. Audience: all engineers. Purpose: awareness — engineers learn what happened elsewhere, even if the lesson does not apply to their systems. Requires no preparation beyond the postmortem.
Sister-team briefing:

A 30-minute sync between the source team's EM and the EMs of teams who own adjacent or similar systems. Audience: EMs of teams that share the failure mode. Purpose: ensure that teams who face the same risk know about it and adapt their runbooks or monitors proactively.
Runbook and alert update:

The postmortem's action items include a runbook or alert change that must be propagated to other teams. The source team authors the canonical change; a Reliability Engineering representative reviews and distributes it to the affected teams' runbook repositories.
All-hands incident review slot:

A 20-minute presentation of the incident at the monthly engineering all-hands. Audience: all engineers. Purpose: build shared mental models about how the system fails at a high level, reinforce psychological safety by modelling blameless discussion publicly. The IC or EM presents; the VP of Engineering facilitates Q&A.
Onboarding module update:

If the lesson reveals a risk that every new engineer needs to understand, it is incorporated into the engineering onboarding module for that system or for the relevant architectural pattern. This is the highest-leverage channel for institutional memory because it reaches every future engineer automatically.
Game day exercise:

A structured chaos engineering exercise that injects the same failure mode into a non-production environment and asks the on-call team to respond as if it were real. Purpose: verify that the process fix (runbook, alert, escalation path) actually works before the next real incident. Game days are expensive — 4–8 engineering hours — and are reserved for score-10 lessons.
Architecture review:

Lessons that challenge architectural assumptions require a structured review of whether the assumption is present elsewhere in the codebase. The Architecture Review Board schedules a one-hour session within 30 days to assess whether a broader change is warranted.
AI-Specific Lesson Rollout Patterns
AI inference services produce incident patterns that benefit from specialised rollout tracks not needed for classical software services.
Lesson Category
Primary Rollout Channel
Additional Audience
Model quality degradation was missed by existing alerts
Alert library update distributed to all model-serving teams
ML Platform team; deployment gate owners
Prompt injection caused unexpected model behaviour
Safety-specific briefing; Red Team review of attack surface
Policy team; all teams that expose model completions to users
New model version caused capacity regression at scale
Game day: capacity test with next candidate model before release
Infra team; ML training team that sets model sizing parameters
Context window edge case produced malformed output at P95
Eval suite updated; test added to deployment gate
Teams building downstream parsers; SDK maintainers
Verifying Knowledge Transfer

A lesson is rolled out when the channel activity is complete. A lesson is landed when the target audience has internalised it and would respond differently to the same situation. The distinction matters because it is easy to log a digest publication and declare victory; it is harder to verify that the on-call engineers in the sister teams actually updated their response patterns.

60 days after rollout, the EM checks three evidence points: whether any similar incident in the affected teams was handled better (shorter MTTD or MTTR, or correct escalation path used), whether the runbook changes were actually applied, and whether the game day (if one was run) produced a result that differed from the original incident. This is not a formal review — it is a 30-minute check that is logged in the postmortem record.
Program Health Metrics

The lessons program itself needs metrics to determine whether it is working. Tracking only activity (number of digests published, number of game days run) measures effort, not impact. The program is healthy when the metrics show that lessons are landing and reducing repeat incidents.
Knowledge landing rate:
Fraction of score-4+ lessons that pass the 60-day verification check. Target ≥ 80%.
Cross-team repeat incident rate:

Fraction of incidents in sister teams that match a failure mode for which a lesson was rolled out within the previous 180 days. A high rate indicates that lessons are not transferring across team boundaries. Target ≤ 10%.
Triage completion rate:
Fraction of postmortems that have a lesson score within 7 days of publication. A low rate indicates the scoring step is being skipped, which breaks the entire program. Target ≥ 95%.
Game day cadence:
Number of score-10 lessons that resulted in a game day within 30 days. Every score-10 lesson must produce a game day — a miss here represents the highest-risk knowledge gap in the organisation.
Rollout Calendar and Scheduling

The lessons program lives or dies by scheduling discipline. If rollout activities are ad-hoc, they get deprioritised during busy periods — which are precisely the periods when high-score incidents occur and lessons are most urgent. A standing rollout calendar with reserved slots protects the program from the sprint pressure that kills most organisational learning initiatives.
Cadence
Activity
Owner
Duration
Weekly
Digest compiled and sent — all postmortems published that week
Reliability Eng or EM rotation
30 min
Monthly
All-hands incident review slot — one score-7+ lesson presented
IC or EM of source team
20 min
Quarterly
Game day — one or two score-10 lessons exercised
Reliability Eng; IC plays observer
4–8 hours
Quarterly
Program health review — metrics, landing rate, gap analysis
VP Engineering
60 min
Annually
Onboarding module refresh — incorporate top 5 lessons from the year
Engineering Enablement team
1 day

The monthly all-hands slot is the highest-visibility activity. It models blameless culture publicly — if senior leaders attend and ask non-blame questions, engineers at every level receive that signal. If the slot is cancelled during a busy month, the signal is that lessons matter less than features under pressure. The slot must be protected.
Designing a Game Day for AI-Specific Failure Modes

A game day for an AI inference failure mode differs structurally from a classic SRE chaos exercise. Classical game days inject infrastructure failures — kill a node, fill a disk, partition the network — and observe whether the on-call team detects and recovers. AI game days must also inject model-layer failures: silently degraded output quality, a context window edge case that only manifests at high load, or a prompt injection vector that is activated by a specific input pattern.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class AIFailureInjection(str, Enum):
    QUALITY_DEGRADATION     = "quality_degradation"    # output quality silently drops
    CONTEXT_WINDOW_OVERFLOW = "context_window_overflow" # edge case at P95 input length
    LATENCY_SPIKE           = "latency_spike"           # GPU memory fragmentation
    ROLLOUT_HALT            = "rollout_halt"            # new model version deploy stuck
    ALERT_SUPPRESSION       = "alert_suppression"       # alert fires but is noise-filtered

@dataclass
class GameDayScenario:
    lesson_postmortem_id: str
    failure_injection: AIFailureInjection
    injection_params: dict          # e.g. {"quality_drop_pct": 15, "start_at_min": 5}
    target_teams: list[str]         # who plays responder
    observer_teams: list[str]       # teams learning by watching
    success_criteria: list[str]     # what the responder must do to pass

    def briefing(self) -> str:
        criteria = "\n".join(f"  - {c}" for c in self.success_criteria)
        return (
            f"Game Day: {self.failure_injection.value}\n"
            f"Postmortem: {self.lesson_postmortem_id}\n"
            f"Responders: {', '.join(self.target_teams)}\n"
            f"Success criteria:\n{criteria}"
        )


# Example: game day for a model quality degradation lesson
quality_game_day = GameDayScenario(
    lesson_postmortem_id="PM-2025-047",
    failure_injection=AIFailureInjection.QUALITY_DEGRADATION,
    injection_params={"quality_drop_pct": 20, "start_at_min": 10, "duration_min": 30},
    target_teams=["serving-team", "product-safety-team"],
    observer_teams=["ml-platform", "api-team"],
    success_criteria=[
        "Quality degradation alert fires within 15 minutes of injection",
        "IC role claimed within 5 minutes of alert",
        "Rollback initiated within 30 minutes of detection",
        "Status page updated before 45-minute mark",
    ],
)

After the game day, the observer teams complete a structured debrief template that covers: what they would have done differently if they had been the responders, whether they noticed any gaps between the lesson that was rolled out to them and the actual response they observed, and what runbook or alert changes they will make to their own systems as a result of watching. The debrief output goes into the lesson verification record.
Handling Sensitive Lessons

Some lessons from major incidents cannot be rolled out through the standard program without modification. Incidents involving user data, model safety violations, or security vulnerabilities may need to be shared in a restricted context before any broader rollout — and some details may never be appropriate to share company-wide.
Security vulnerability lessons:

If the lesson describes an exploit vector that has not yet been fully closed, the rollout is restricted to the teams that need to close it. A broader rollout happens only after the vulnerability is mitigated. Legal must sign off on any written summary that describes how an attack worked.
User data incidents:

Lessons from incidents that involved user data must be anonymised before rollout — no conversation content, no user identifiers, no account names. The lesson is the system failure and the fix, not the details of what data was involved.
Model safety incidents:

Lessons from incidents where the model produced outputs that violated safety guidelines are rolled out through a restricted track: the Policy team and the Responsible Scaling team are briefed before any broader sharing. The all-hands slot may summarise the incident at a high level without disclosing the specific prompt or output pattern that triggered the event.
Legal hold:

If a major incident is the subject of a regulatory investigation or litigation, Legal may place a hold on the postmortem and restrict the lessons rollout entirely until the investigation is resolved. Engineering teams must treat a Legal hold notice for an incident as an immediate halt to all rollout activities for that incident.
Summary

The major incident lessons program converts individual postmortems into organisation-wide learning through a structured triage and rollout process. Each postmortem is scored on novelty, blast radius, and applicability — a total score of 1–12 that determines which rollout channels are activated: from a digest entry for low-score lessons to a mandatory game day and architecture review for score-10 events. AI-specific lessons (model quality degradation detection gaps, prompt injection surface, capacity regression patterns) route to specialised channels including safety policy briefing and eval suite updates. Landing verification at 60 days checks whether runbooks were adopted, whether similar incidents were handled better, and whether game days passed. The program is healthy when the cross-team repeat incident rate is below 10% and at least 80% of score-4+ lessons pass verification.
